hero.title:
The Silent
Rhythm.
hero.subtitle: A refined operating surface for modern restaurant teams.
hero.supporting: Built for restaurants with European ambition.

features.desc: MAÎTRISE Atelier brings presentation, control, and guest experience into one structured system — a premium digital system for sharper control and world-class guest experience.
features.arch_desc: Maximum simplicity in use, minimal latency in execution. We engineer high-fidelity systems built on architectural precision and modernist principles.
features.integrity_desc: Check out the restaurant menu control panel. You can also view AI-generated dish stories and photos here.
features.neural_desc: Experience the simplicity and complexity of our system through a restaurant visit simulation.
features.step1_desc: Systemic auditing of existing digital assets to identify structural vulnerabilities and optimization vectors.
features.step2_desc: Fabrication of proprietary operational frameworks built on high-fidelity modernist logic and ultra-clean code.
features.step3_desc: Full-scale deployment with architectural precision, ensuring peak performance across the digital ecosystem.

transition.desc1: No templates. A system designed for a specific restaurant only.
transition.desc2: Interface, operation and space control — fully managed processes and flows.

process.title:
The Architecture of
Invisible Service

steps.title: Sensing
steps.desc: Advanced transaction mapping that understands the rhythm of the floor before the guest arrives.

steps.title: Flow
steps.desc: Interactions so seamless they disappear into the service flow, empowering staff without distraction.

steps.title: Legacy
steps.desc: Automating the final transaction touchpoint to ensure the lasting memory is the experience.

architecture.phil_desc: Service that anticipates rather than reacts. We engineer digital touchpoints that disappear into the atmosphere, leaving only the experience.
architecture.box1_desc: Interfaces that adapt to intent before a query is formed.
architecture.box2_desc: Tools that exist functionally within the negative space of hospitality.
architecture.tech1_desc: The evolution of the QR code. A frictionless gateway that recognizes context—time, table, and guest history—delivering a hyper-personalized digital concierge instantly.
architecture.tech2_desc: Centralized, absolute intelligence. Disparate systems synthesized into a singular, elegant dashboard that empowers staff with actionable foresight.
architecture.tech3_desc: Zero-latency integration between front-of-house intent and back-of-house execution. The architecture operates entirely behind the veil.

manifesto.title: We do not do easy.
manifesto.desc1: Standard is the enemy of the exceptional. We don't build websites; we engineer gravity.
manifesto.desc2: For restaurants that treat service as craft, not content. MAÎTRISE Atelier brings design discipline, operational control, and quiet digital precision to the guest experience.

footer.tagline: Architects of high-end hospitality digital infrastructure.

floorSync.title: The Spatial Protocol
floorSync.subtitle: Experience the frictionless connection between the guest's mobile device and the kitchen's real-time floor board. No waiters, no delays. Just absolute command.
floorSync.qrTitle: Open the live floor demo on real phones
floorSync.qrSubtitle: These QR codes point to this computer session. If you already changed the menu in Control Panel, guests will open that local version. Otherwise the standard menu is used for this session.
floorSync.qrNote: Scan on mobile. The scan route immediately redirects into /floor-sync with the correct table and shared session.
floorSync.wineDesc: Deep, dark, and complex. Decanted 30 minutes prior to pouring.
floorSync.winterCollectionDesc: Seasonal menu reflecting the beauty and richness of Georgia's nature. Each dish is a work of art created with the finest ingredients and traditional recipes.

ui.focusHint: Click a table to inspect its live feed.

overview.title: Commercial control, not just a menu feed.
overview.description: This workspace combines one live operational tab with two modeled intelligence tabs so you can demonstrate what the finished restaurant system will feel like.
overview.framingBody: Keep the live board grounded in the current session. Use the insights and AI tabs to sell the future commercial value.
overview.storyTitle: What this dashboard can communicate in one meeting

insights.title: Action-oriented insights
insights.description: This tab shows how the product stops being a menu and becomes a decision tool. Every card below is demonstrational and intentionally modeled.

ai.title: AI interpretation
ai.description: The AI role here is simple: translate behavioral data into clear recommendations, summaries, and experiments that operators can act on.

scan.title: Scan QR to add another participant
scan.description: This tab is only for the presentation. It is not part of the regular dashboard product. Use it to bring one more phone into the same demo session.
scan.active: Already active in the demo, but still available if you want to reuse it.
steps: 1. Open this tab during the presentation.
2. Ask another participant to scan the code on their phone.
3. Their actions will appear inside the same live demonstration session.
scan.qrDescription: Scans into the current dashboard demo session and opens the guest route directly on mobile.
scan.note: Not a regular dashboard feature. This exists only so you can add another participant during the demo.

panels.title: Live operations board
panels.description: Keep the current floor visualization intact so stakeholders still see real guest activity.

panels.title: Action-oriented insights
panels.description: Show how behavior patterns become concrete menu and placement decisions.

panels.title: AI interpretation
panels.description: Demonstrate summaries, anomaly detection, and plain-language recommendations.

notes: These intelligence tabs are demonstrational. Their numbers are modeled, not pulled from live sessions.
The goal is to show owners, chefs, and managers what the finished product layer can look like.
Real analytics can later replace every modeled card without changing the interface structure.

moves.title: Reframe {primaryDish}
moves.detail: Treat it as a presentation problem first, not a demand problem.

moves.title: Promote {dessert}
moves.detail: Introduce desserts earlier so guests see them before mentally closing the meal.

moves.title: Use {mains} as a control block
moves.detail: It can anchor A/B tests because it already attracts stable intent.

moves.title: Separate tourist behavior by time
moves.detail: Model a dedicated 20:00 to 22:30 visitor path for non-local language users.

insights.title: {spotlight} receives the heaviest attention between 19:00 and 21:00.
insights.description: Guests open this section early in dinner service, then return to it after first-round drinks.
insights.action: Move this category into the first visible row during peak hours.

insights.title: {primaryDish} earns many opens but lags behind {secondaryDish} in follow-through.
insights.description: The item looks interesting enough to inspect, but the current presentation does not close the decision.
insights.action: Refresh the photo first, then test a tighter description.

insights.title: Russian-language visitors stay longer inside menu details than English-language visitors.
insights.description: That usually signals stronger evaluation behavior, not necessarily stronger intent to order.
insights.action: Use shorter decision cues for English and deeper detail blocks for Russian.

insights.title: Tables 4 and 8 show the highest repeat digital engagement.
insights.description: These tables reopen dish details more often and spend longer in comparison behavior.
insights.action: Use these tables for placement experiments and higher-margin upsells.

insights.title: {secondaryDish} underperforms despite stronger placement than {primaryDish}.
insights.description: Visibility alone is not enough when the item story and first image are weaker than the alternative.
insights.action: Swap copy or photography before allocating more prime placement.

insights.title: {dessert} receives strong late-session attention but stays too hidden on first entry.
insights.description: Guests discover sweet options deep into the journey, after most primary decisions are already made.
insights.action: Bring desserts forward earlier in the browsing path or after mains.

showcase.aiSummary: This week, {mains} gained strong attention from English-language visitors after 20:00. {dessert} detail views increased late in the journey, but conversion signals stayed flat. Consider surfacing desserts earlier and revising the presentation of {primaryDish} and {dessertDish}.

aiCapabilities.title: Weekly summary
aiCapabilities.description: Turn raw behavior into a short operator briefing written in plain language.

aiCapabilities.title: Anomaly detection
aiCapabilities.description: Flag unusual drops, spikes, or sudden attention shifts before teams notice manually.

aiCapabilities.title: Underperforming dishes
aiCapabilities.description: Separate weak presentation from weak demand by comparing opens against follow-through.

aiCapabilities.title: A/B interpretation
aiCapabilities.description: Explain whether featured slots, copy changes, or new photos actually improved behavior.

aiCapabilities.title: Seasonality spotting
aiCapabilities.description: Surface cyclical shifts by daypart, week, or visitor type without spreadsheet work.

aiCapabilities.title: Language behavior
aiCapabilities.description: Compare how different language groups inspect categories, details, and menu depth.

aiPrompts.question: Which dishes attract attention but probably fail to convert?
aiPrompts.answer: {primaryDish} stands out as the clearest presentation issue. Guests inspect it often, but modeled add-to-cart behavior is weak compared with {secondaryDish}. {dessertDish} shows a similar pattern later in the session, suggesting curiosity without enough confidence to act.
actions: Shorten the first two lines of the description to make the choice easier.
Retest {dessertDish} with an earlier placement and a tighter name card.

aiPrompts.question: Which categories should we move higher?
aiPrompts.answer: {spotlight} and {dessert} both deserve earlier visibility. The first attracts peak-hour attention quickly, while the second earns late-session interest once guests finally discover it.
actions: Move {spotlight} into the first visible row at dinner launch.
Use one featured slot for a short high-margin category teaser.

aiPrompts.question: When do tourists interact most?
aiPrompts.answer: Modeled tourist engagement climbs after 20:00 and stays strongest until roughly 22:30. That period shows the highest concentration of language switching, deep detail inspection, and comparison behavior.
actions: Schedule multilingual featured items for the evening window.
Show shorter decision cues early and richer stories one tap deeper.
Use this period for premium upsell experiments instead of lunch.

aiPrompts.question: Which tables have the most digital engagement?
aiPrompts.answer: Tables 4 and 8 produce the most modeled digital engagement. They reopen items more often, compare alternatives longer, and react more consistently to featured placements than the other demo tables.
actions: Prioritize these tables for placement experiments.
Watch whether staff service style changes the interaction depth.
Use their patterns as a baseline when new promotional logic is introduced.

aiPrompts.question: Did the new featured items perform better than the previous ones?
aiPrompts.answer: The new featured slot increases visibility, but the improvement is uneven. {secondaryDish} gains placement advantage without enough response, while {primaryDish} still wins on pure attention even from a weaker slot.
actions: Keep the experiment running, but swap the weaker featured creative.
Judge the slot by attention-to-action ratio, not by impressions alone.
Retain the winning story structure from the better-performing item.

aiPrompts.question: Which language users are more likely to inspect details?
aiPrompts.answer: Russian-language visitors show the deepest modeled detail-inspection behavior. English-language visitors decide faster, but they also abandon detail pages sooner when the first image or first sentence is weak.
actions: Give Russian flows richer context and pairing suggestions.
Keep English flows sharper, faster, and visually decisive.
Use language-specific summaries rather than one universal description style.
